head_coach.name: Aaron Glenn
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Aggressive, man-coverage-heavy defense built on physicality, pressure, and versatile safety play. Played under Bill Parcells and Bill Belichick; coached under Dennis Allen in New Orleans and alongside Dan Campbell in Detroit. Will call the defensive plays himself in 2026. CEO-style manager on offense, delegates playcalling to OC.

offensive_coordinator.name: Frank Reich
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: West Coast / Erhardt-Perkins hybrid; Peyton Manning Colts tree, Doug Pederson/Eagles influence

defensive_coordinator.name: Brian Duker
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Aaron Glenn / Detroit Lions tree; Baltimore Ravens background under John Harbaugh; secondary specialist

offense.scheme_family: West Coast with modern RPO elements
offense.personnel_tendencies: Reich averaged 72% 11 personnel across his last six seasons as HC; 87% in Carolina 2023, 77% in Indianapolis 2022. Remainder is primarily 12 personnel. Virtually no 21/22 personnel usage (3% and 0.5% respectively). Expect 65-80% 11 personnel.
offense.run_scheme: Inside zone / man-duo primary; outside zone used sparingly, well below league average. Between-the-tackles emphasis.
offense.pass_concepts: Triangle reads over the middle of the field, short-to-intermediate emphasis. Favors dig/in routes and drag routes at league-leading rates. Below-average go/post route usage. No-huddle and tempo elements. Play action usage is variable (high under Luck, low in recent years). Screens and YAC-oriented concepts. Aggressive on fourth down.
offense.te_usage: Primary TE in 11 personnel as inline blocker with receiving role; TEs accounted for 37.2% of Colts TD receptions under Reich (league avg 28.5%). When in 12 personnel, TEs flex and create mismatches. TE is a red zone weapon, not a pure move piece.
offense.rb_usage: Bell cow with significant receiving role. Colts RBs averaged 117 targets and 663 receiving yards per season under Reich. Pass-catching ability highly valued.

defense.alignment: Even front (4-down) primary; capable of mixing in odd fronts (3-3-5 nickel) seamlessly. Glenn ran 4-3 as his base in all four Detroit seasons but used tite fronts and 3-man looks situationally. Nickel is the true base given modern NFL 11-personnel rates.
defense.edge_rush_source: 4-3 DE hands-in-dirt primary; DEs may occasionally stand up. Edge rushers are traditional defensive ends, not OLB stand-up types.
defense.coverage_base: Man-heavy (Cover 1 primary, 35%+ usage in Detroit 2024), with quarters/split-field zone concepts mixed in. Two-man coverage influence from Saints tenure. Aggressive man-to-man approach, corners expected to play on islands. Glenn evolved from zone-heavy early in Detroit to man-dominant once he acquired the right personnel.
defense.blitz_tendency: heavy (Glenn blitzed on 56% of dropbacks in key games; extensive Cover 0 usage; described as aggressive blitzer who uses variety of blitz packages including double edge rushers, DL twists, safety blitzes, and delayed rushes)
defense.lb_role: Traditional run-and-coverage LBs in a 4-3 scheme. ILBs are communicators and box defenders, not primary pass rushers. Glenn values LBs who can cover and fit the run. Hybrid LBs who can handle slot coverage in base packages are prized.
defense.safety_usage: Extremely high value on safety play. Glenn built the Lions' defense around the Kerby Joseph / Brian Branch safety tandem. One safety plays the post (deep), one plays down/box as a versatile man-coverage / slot defender. Safety who can play nickel-slot-box roles and communicate coverages is critical. Glenn's system requires safeties to handle multiple coverage responsibilities and occasionally blitz.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Reich's offense historically features TEs as meaningful red zone targets (37.2% of TD receptions in Indy, vs 28.5% league avg), and TEs are involved in 12-personnel passing concepts with triangle reads and play action. However, his extreme 11 personnel lean (72% career avg) means the TE1 is often the only TE on the field, limiting multi-TE receiving opportunities. The TE functions more as an inline weapon with receiving upside than a flexed-out move piece. A receiving TE adds value, but not at WR-level premium.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Glenn runs a 4-3 even front where edge rush comes from hands-in-dirt defensive ends, not stand-up OLBs. ILBs in this system are run-coverage players, not primary pass rushers. An LB with elite pass rush traits has limited positional value here compared to a true 3-4 system. Glenn may occasionally use an LB in a blitz package, but the primary pass rush identity is built around 4-3 DEs. LB pass rush traits are a situational bonus, not a scheme-driven value multiplier.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Reich's offenses have historically been among the league's most RB-friendly in the passing game. Colts RBs averaged 117 targets and 663 receiving yards per season from 2018-22. His West Coast roots emphasize getting the ball to playmakers within 5 yards of the LOS and generating YAC. The emphasis on screens (top 8 in Indy), short passing, and RB route-running makes a pass-catching back significantly more valuable in this scheme than in a pure ground-and-pound system. Breece Hall's receiving upside is a major asset.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Glenn's defensive philosophy is built around elite safety play. He explicitly values safeties who can play multiple coverage roles, comparing his ideal tandem to Malcolm Jenkins/Marcus Williams in New Orleans and Kerby Joseph/Brian Branch in Detroit. His system requires one safety as a deep post player and another as a down/box/slot defender who can play man coverage, fit the run, and blitz. Brian Branch was effectively used as a safety-nickel hybrid in Detroit, and Glenn's Cover 0 blitz packages frequently incorporate safety pressure. A safety with versatility (box, slot, blitz, man coverage) gets a major value boost in this scheme. Glenn invested premium draft capital in safeties throughout his Detroit tenure.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: The Jets' defensive rebuild under Glenn prioritizes three archetypes: (1) 4-3 DEs with elite first-step quickness and pass-rush ability — Glenn needs edge talent desperately after trading Quinnen Williams and losing Sauce Gardner; (2) physical, press-capable CBs who can play man on islands — Glenn's system demands cornerbacks who thrive without safety help; (3) hybrid safeties who can toggle between deep coverage, slot coverage, and box/blitz roles — this is Glenn's highest-value positional investment historically. On offense, Reich's 11-personnel-heavy West Coast scheme with inside zone run concepts means: WRs who win on intermediate in-breaking routes (dig, drag) are prioritized over pure vertical threats; RBs with receiving chops are highly valued; TEs need to be well-rounded (block in inside zone, run routes from inline, score in red zone). The Jets hold the No. 2 overall pick and need a franchise QB — Reich's adaptability to different QB types (pocket passer or dual-threat) is a strength. For positional value mapping: do NOT boost LB-to-EDGE for NYJ (4-3 scheme), DO boost RB receiving and safety versatility significantly, and apply moderate TE receiving boost given red zone emphasis.
